mnemonics.title: B+ Tree Benefits
mnemonics.content: B+ Tree: Balanced Plus - All Data at Leaves, Plus linked leaves for range queries, Balanced for consistent performance

mnemonics.title: Index Types Memory
mnemonics.content: P-Primary, C-Clustering, S-Secondary (PCCS): Primary=Primary Key, Clustering=Non-key similar values, Secondary=Additional access paths

mnemonics.title: Dense vs Sparse
mnemonics.content: Dense=Every Record, Sparse=Some Records - DENSE has more entries, SPARSE saves space

acronyms.usage: Self-balancing tree data structure used for indexing with sorted data at all levels
Enhanced B-tree where all data is at leaf nodes, optimized for range queries
Software for creating and managing databases including indexing capabilities
Person responsible for managing indexes, among other database tasks
Language used to create and manage indexes in databases

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted tree with root at top, branches spreading downward. Leaf nodes at bottom should be shown as horizontal linked boxes. All key-value pairs visible at leaf level only.
Picture a book with chapters (first level), sections within chapters (second level), and pages (actual data). Each level narrows down to specific location.
Full scan = flashlight scanning entire room; Index = GPS taking direct route to target location.
Clustering = clothing organized by type (shirts together, pants together); Non-clustering = catalog/索引 showing where items are located anywhere in store.

analogies.analogy: Like a book index at the back - instead of reading every page to find a topic, you use the index to jump directly to the right page.

analogies.concept: B+ Tree Leaf Nodes Linked List
analogies.analogy: Like train cars connected together - once you find the first car (first matching record), you can easily traverse to all subsequent cars without returning to the station (root).
Like maintaining a detailed table of contents in a notebook - when you add a new page, you must update the table of contents, taking extra time.
Like a directory showing floor numbers in an apartment building rather than listing every apartment number - you know which floor, then search within that floor.
Like a telephone directory sorted by last name, then first name - you can find people by last name, or last+first, but not by first name alone.
